system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 2: Jazz Standard Study #1: Autumn Leaves
Pages 20-38 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Let’s start diving into these essential jazz standards! | have
each of these studies in a specific order because | believe
the lessons learned build on each other. However, you can
still jump between them as you wish.

Keep in mind that these songs have valuable lessons to
teach us about jazz repertoire and music in general. By ana-
lyzing and learning from these, we can apply these concepts
to just about any other jazz standard.

These jazz standards were picked for our studies because of
the melodic, harmonic, and rhythmic information they pos-
sess. Here are the things we will cover in each jazz standard
study:

v¥ A brief bio of the song.

Y¥ Suggested recordings to listen to.

v AChords Map to identify the chords and chord tones.
¥ An in-depth analysis of the harmony.

v A Guide Tones chart.

¥ Aunique Improv Lesson to take away from it.

I’m excited to jump into these, and | know you are too. So
let’s not waste time. Let’s dive right in!



Autumn Leaves is an excellent jazz standard for us to start with be-
cause it covers some basic chord progressions and harmonic con-
cepts found in jazz. In fact, a lot of jazz musicians start out on Autumn
leaves for these very reasons.

We are going to spend a little bit of extra time on this one because
there are many small lessons we need to cover that will set us up for
success throughout the rest of the jazz standard studies.

Qutumn feawes Bie

Autumn Leaues was composed by the Hungarian-French Composer Joseph Kos-
ma with lyrics by poet Jacques Prévert. Its original title is “Les Feuilles Mortes”
which means “The Dead Leaves.” American songwriter Johnny Mercer wrote
English lyrics.

The song has an AABA form, meaning the first theme (or section) is repeated,
followed by the second theme, and ending on the original theme. It has two
common played keys: concert G minor and E minor.

Autumn Leaves is arguably one of the most popular jazz standards of all time,
and has been recorded many hundreds of times.

CONCERT KE4S AND TRANSPOSING FOR YouR INSTRUMENT

NOTE: Whenever | talk about “common keys” throughout this
book, | am referring to the concert keys that jazz musicians most
commonly play these jazz standards in. These are not necessarily
the original keys, but the keys you will need to know these songs in
if you want to play them with other musicians


The last thing | want to mention regarding this is regardless of what in-
strument you play, you should know what the econeert key is. Depend-
ing on what version of this book you purchased, it has been left original
or transposed to fit the key of your instrument.

So if you are a Bb tenor sax player, the key you will be looking at Au-
tumn Leaves in is A minor (a whole step up from the concert key). If
you are an Eb alto sax player, you will be seeing E minor (a minor 3%
down from the concert key). If you are a C instrument like piano or gui-
tar, you will be playing in G minor as is.

The takeaway here is if at a gig someone calls Autumn Leaves in con-
cert G minor, make sure you know what that means for you and your
instrument.

>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Frank Sinatra from “Where are You?” 1957

Cannonball Adderley from “Somethin’ Else” 1958

Bill Evans Trio from “Portraits in Jazz” 1959

Gene Ammons and Sonny Stitt from “Boss Tenors”
1961

(Note on this recording there is some added harmonic movement out-
side of the original chord changes)

Let’s take a look at the chords map, which includes the chord chang-
es, the chord tones in each chord, and a basic piano voicing and guitar
voicing. Whether you play piano or guitar or not, this resource can be
helpful to have available to you. You can listen to this by finding the
track in your download folder or simply by clicking the image.


AUTUMN LEAVES

The main things | want you to get out of the chord maps in this book
are:

1. What the chord changes are.
2. What the chord tones that make up each chord are.

3. Basic piano or guitar voicings. The piano voicings are especially
helpful even if you aren’t a piano player. Basic piano skills can be
useful for any instrumentalist.

A CRASH COURSE ON HOW CHORD PROGRESSIONS WORK

In a few pages, we’re going to dig in deeper to Autumn Leaves with
a chords analysis. But | would be doing you a disservice if | didn’t
quickly talk about how to build chord progressions. If you don’t un-
derstand this on a fundamental level, the chords analysis may be
confusing.

I’m not going to go into too much depth on this, as the theory be-
hind building chords and chord progressions isn’t a primary focus in
this book. If you don’t fully understand some of these basics, | would
suggest checking out my other book Zero to Improv to accompany
your studies here.


Take a look at this chart:

MAJOR DIATONIC SERIES OF 7TH CHORDS

Cc D E F G A B
Maj7 Min7 Min7 Maj7 Dom7 Min7 Halfdim
Cmaj7 =Dmin7 ~—_Emin7 Fmaj7 G7 Amin7 Bmin7(b5)

| ii iii IV V vi vii(b5)

This chart is called the Major Diatonic Series of 7* Chords. Essen-
tially, it shows you how chords can be assigned to the different scale
degrees of a Major scale.

¥ The top line shows the note names spelling out a C major scale.

Vv The second line shows the quality of chord created when a 7*
chord is harmonized with each scale tone.

VY The third line shows the entire 7** chord name.

¥ The fourth line shows the roman numerals for each scale degree.
The upper case means major or dominant, and lower case means
minor. Ultimately you just need to memorize the quality of chord
for each scale tone.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



We can create chord progressions simply by combining different 7%
chords in the diatonic series. For example, here’s a ii-V-| chord progres-
sion in C major.

7 7 7
Dain G Cuny

6}: fi. ff. ff. ff. ff. ff. fi. ff. ff. ff. ff. fi.

If you look back up at your Major Diatonic Series of 7* Chords chart,
take a look at what the ii, V, and | chord are. You'll see that they are
indeed Dmin7, G7, and Cmaj/.

Try making a handful of chord progressions yourself. What's a I-vi-ii-V? Or
a I-IV-iii-vi? This will be important for you to understand going forward.

Now, this is how you can derive chord progressions for major keys, but
there is also another chart for minor keys:

MINOR DIATONIC SERIES OF 7TH CHORDS

(65 D Eb F G A Bb
Min7 Halfdim Maj7 Min7 Dom7  Halfdim Dom7
Cmin7 Dmin7(b5) Ebmaj7  Fmin7 G7 Amin7(b5) Bb7

| ii(b5) lll iV V vi(b5) Vil

This is the Minor Diatonic Series of 7% Chords. It shows you how 7*
chords can be assigned to the different scale degrees of a minor scale.
In this particular case, the natural minor scale is being harmonized, al-
though the 5‘ degree borrows a note from the harmonic and melodic
minor scales. This is because the V chord is always dominant.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 28 |



IMPORTANT NOTE: [he Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords
is different from the Major Diatonic Series in that it more so rep-
resents common chord choices in the minor tonality than an actual
harmonized scale. It is much more complicated to come up with
a true scale to harmonize because there are the three different
minor scales to consider. This becomes problematic when trying
to provide only one set of roman numeral scale degrees. An entire
chapter could be dedicated to this, but for now for simplicities sake,
understand there are borrowed tones from the different scales, re-
sulting in an imperfect harmonization

Take a look at that same ii-V-i chord progression but in C minor.

5)

6y: —p Z Zz Zz Zz Zz Z Z Zz Z Z Zz Z

If you take a look at the chart, it all checks out. The ii chord is Dmin7b5
(also known as half-diminished), the V chord is G7, and the | chord is
Cmin7.

You may want to refer to these charts throughout these jazz stan-
dard studies if you don’t have a strong understanding of how chord
progressions work. However, the more you work with jazz standards
and study the chord analysis’ | will give you, the more this will naturally
make sense.

Now, let’s jump into our first chord analysis of this book. Take a good
look at it, and then I’m going to start breaking it down so that we can
understand how the harmonic structure of Autumn Leaves works.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 29 |

As you can see, | have color-coded different sections of the form to
help you understand what is going on with the chords and chord pro-
gressions. The colors will not always mean the same things in each
study, so let me quickly identify them:

‘Blue = the relative major key.
Red = the minor parent key.
Green =a section of cycling 4ths.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



UNDERSTANDING KELATIVE KES

The first thing | want to observe about Autumn Leaves is how it shifts
between the relative major and minor keys.

>> WHAT ARE RELATIVE KEYS?

Major and minor keys that share the same key signature are called rel-
ative keys. Essentially, they share the exact same notes as each other.

Autumn Leaves is in the key of G minor. Take a look at the G minor
scale.

a5 : :

Vv

From the minor key you can either memorize its relative key or use this
trick: think a major key a minor 3% up from the minor key (or vise-versa).
In the case of G minor what would that be? Bb major.

nah.
Te

ND
Sa

Te

Here is a Bb major scale, and as you can see, they share the exact
same key signature. Fairly simple, right?

Autumn Leaves is the perfect study of how relative major and minor
keys work together. The entire song is switching between the two, ex-
cept one section. Let’s take a look at it.


Guin’? c’ Fun’ BY”

-— Minor I-IV7 ii-V of Relative IV
(Cycle Of Fourths) --------------------

If there is any slightly confusing part of Autumn Leaves, this would be it.
However, it’s not really all that strange when it comes to jazz harmony.

It starts out on the root minor and moves to a dominant IV chord. The
movement of the root notes in 4ths is very common in jazz harmony, and
we'll go over that more next. The IV chord in a minor key is usually minor,
but jazz musicians are constantly turning minor chords into dominant 7
chords. You'll see that happen a lot more throughout our studies!

From there, you can consider the second bar a ii-V chord progression
to the relative major IV chord (Eb), although it doesn’t resolve.

| personally find it beneficial to think of both of those bars as isolated
ii-V chord progressions with no attachment to any particular key. Af-
ter all, they are just passing chords that ultimately resolve to a minor
ii-V-i of G minor.

MAJOR AND MINOR I-V-1 CHORD PROGRESSIONS

The second big thing to observe about Autumn Leaves is that it is
almost entirely ii-V-| chord progressions both in the minor and relative
major key.

When it comes to jazz, there is no more important chord progression
to know than the ii-V-l. You will see it come up time and time again,
and so you need to be able to recognize it right away, and of course,

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



know how to improvise over it. The ii-V-| is the cornerstone chord pro-
gression in jazz.

Autumn Leaves starts out with a ii-V-I in the relative major key.

Cun’ F’ Blas”

ii-V-I of Relative Major ------------------------------

This is where knowing your Major and Minor Diatonic Series of 7*
Chords comes in handy. You will want to know how to transpose ii-V-I’s
into all 12 keys.

If you know them well, you can quickly identify that Bb is the relative
major of G minor and that Cmin7-F7-Blbmaj7 is a ii-V-l in the key of Bb
major.

Let’s take a look at the transition into the parent minor key.

4

Ewes” Am7®) D’ Gun’

*  IVof Relative Major ~—- Minor ii-V-I_ - - - -- ---------------------------------------.

The Ebmaj7 is the IV chord of the relative major, and it serves as a
transition chord into the parent minor ii-V-i.

The beauty of Autumn Leaves (and the reason | start with it), is that
it is relatively simple when it comes to jazz harmony all while covering
two central harmonic concepts.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 3a |



IMPROV LESSON
Guide Tones

Throughout this book, we will be covering one improv lesson for every
jazz standard that we cover. These lessons will often draw upon cer-
tain elements within each jazz standard or will use the song as a vehi-
cle for teaching an improvisational concept.

The lesson | want to cover for Autumn Leaves is an important one:
guide tones.

| will be providing guide tone charts for every single jazz standard from
here on out, so | think it’s worth covering for our first improv lesson.

>> WHAT ARE GUIDE TONES?

Guide tones are notes within a chord structure that both help define a
chord, and can be used to transition to another chord melodically.

I’m going to explain that further in just a second, but first, why should
you care about guide tones and how will they help you in your jazz im-
provisation?

One of the most common questions | get is “how do | make the chord
changes come out in my solos?”

In other words, if you started improvising without accompaniment
would a listener know you were playing Autumn Leaves (assuming they
are familiar with the song)?

The key to achieving this is knowing how to outline the chord changes
in your improvisation. In future lessons, we will be digging deeper into
this, but guide tones are the perfect starting point.

In jazz, the guide tones are almost always the 3rds and 7ths of each
chord.


Let’s take a look at a ii-V-| in C major for example:

Dain? G’ Cun’

» 7th 3rd 7th
rae o = =
a o o =

3rd 7th 3rd

The 3rds and 7ths define the chords in this progression. 7“ chords are
built: Root-3'9-5%-7". The root is never altered, and the 5“ is never al-
tered unless it is a half-diminished or fully diminished 7% chord. Even
still, the 5% is not a strong note in the chord. But the 3% and 7* are
pivotal in defining whether a chord is major, minor, dominant, half-di-
minished, or fully diminished.

Now the next example shows this exact same progression with the
guide tones played melodically and with voice leading.

>> WHAT IS VOICE LEADING?

Voice leading is the smooth melodic movement of notes (or voices)
from one chord to the next.

Dain? G’ Cun’

o

hap

3rd 7th 3rd fb 3rd 7th

Notice how the 7* of each chord resolves to the 3 of the next chord.
It happens to work perfectly this way because the chords are cycling
in 4ths. But the basic concept is the closest step-wise motion you can
make from the 7‘ of a chord in this ii-V-l, is the 3 of the next. That’s
voice leading.

Try playing through this on your instrument. You can hear those chord
changes coming out clearly. If you can map out the guide tones of any
jazz standard you are learning, you can identify the notes that will de-
fine the sound of each chord most clearly.



Now, guide tones by themselves are not musical of course. But they
are a starting point for bringing the chord changes out in your solos.
It's a great practice to play them isolated with either half notes or
quarter notes through the entire form of a song, and so that is precise-
ly what we are going to do.

On the next page, the guide tones are mapped out over the entire
form of Autumn Leaves. Play through this and give it a try!
